# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark-construction details, benchmark-scale statistics, evaluation protocol details, baseline model results, retrieval/context analyses, fine-tuning setup details, and stated limitations for SWE-bench as a software-engineering benchmark built from real GitHub issues and pull requests.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: SWE-bench abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench contains 2,294 software engineering task instances drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories.

## Source Excerpt

"We introduce SWE-bench, an evaluation framework consisting of 2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories."

## Note

This is a benchmark composition claim from the paper’s abstract and benchmark description.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Section 2.1 benchmark construction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench task construction uses a 3-stage pipeline: repo selection and PR scraping, attribute-based filtering, and execution-based filtering.

## Source Excerpt

"To find high-quality task instances at scale, we use a 3-stage pipeline as follows." The stages are then listed as "Stage I: Repo selection and data scraping", "Stage II: Attribute-based filtering", and "Stage III: Execution-based filtering."

## Note

The paper presents this as the core benchmark-construction method.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Section 2.1 repo scraping statistics
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The authors initially collected about 90,000 pull requests from 12 popular open-source Python repositories before filtering.

## Source Excerpt

"We start by collecting pull requests (PRs) from 12 popular open-source Python repositories on GitHub, producing about ∼90,000 PRs in total."

## Note

This describes the pre-filtering scale of the raw source data.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Section 2.1 attribute and execution filtering
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Candidate tasks were limited to merged pull requests that resolve a GitHub issue, modify test files, include at least one fail-to-pass test, and do not produce installation or runtime errors.

## Source Excerpt

"Stage II: Attribute-based filtering. We create candidate tasks by selecting the merged PRs that (1) resolve a GitHub issue and (2) make changes to the test files of the repository." "We filter out task instances without at least one test where its status changes from a fail to pass... We also filter out instances that result in installation or runtime errors."

## Note

This combines the explicit filtering criteria across stages II and III.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Section 2.1 final benchmark size
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The filtering pipeline reduced roughly 90,000 pull requests to 2,294 task instances.

## Source Excerpt

"Through these stages of filtering, the original 90,000 PRs are filtered down to the 2,294 task instances which comprise SWE-bench."

## Note

This states the scale reduction from raw PRs to final benchmark tasks.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Section 2.2 evaluation metrics
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
A model solution is counted as successful only if the generated patch applies successfully and all associated unit and system tests pass.

## Source Excerpt

"If the patch applies successfully and all of these tests pass we consider the proposed solution to have successfully resolved the issue. The metric for our benchmark is the percentage of task instances that are resolved."

## Note

This defines the benchmark success criterion and headline metric.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Section 2.3 dataset statistics
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench issue descriptions average 195 words, non-test codebases average 3,010 files and 438K lines, and reference solutions average edits to 1.7 files, 3.0 functions, and 32.8 lines.

## Source Excerpt

"Issue descriptions are typically long and detailed (195 words on average), and codebases regularly contain many thousands of files." Table 1 reports: "Issue Text Length (Words) 195.1", "Codebase # Files (non-test) 3,010", "# Lines (non-test) 438K", and "Gold Patch # Lines edited 32.8", "# Files edited 1.7", "# Func. edited 3".

## Note

These are summary statistics for benchmark instance size and edit scope.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: metric
citation: Section 2.3 robust evaluation statistics
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each task instance has at least one fail-to-pass test, 40% of instances have at least two fail-to-pass tests, and the median instance runs 51 additional tests to check preserved functionality.

## Source Excerpt

"For each task instance, there is at least one fail-to-pass test... and 40% of instances have at least two fail-to-pass tests." "In addition, a median of 51 additional tests run to check whether prior functionality is properly maintained."

## Note

These statistics describe the benchmark’s test coverage and preservation checks.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Table 5 SWE-bench baseline results
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Under BM25 retrieval on the full SWE-bench benchmark, Claude 2 resolved 1.97% of tasks, GPT-4-turbo 1.31%, ChatGPT-3.5 0.17%, SWE-Llama 7b 0.70%, and SWE-Llama 13b 0.70%.

## Source Excerpt

Table 5 reports for SWE-bench: "Claude 2 1.97", "ChatGPT-3.5 0.17", "GPT-4-turbo 1.31", "SWE-Llama 7b 0.70", and "SWE-Llama 13b 0.70" under "% Resolved."

## Note

The abstract rounds Claude 2’s result to 1.96%; Table 5 reports 1.97%.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: claim-support
citation: Section 5 best model performance
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper states that current evaluated models struggle broadly on SWE-bench, with the best-performing model solving only about 2% of issues under the main BM25 setting.

## Source Excerpt

"Across the board, models struggle significantly to resolve issues. The best performing model, Claude 2, is only able to resolve 1.96% of the issues."

## Note

This is a summary interpretation attached to reported benchmark results; the exact percentage varies slightly between abstract text and Table 5 (1.96% vs. 1.97%).
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: result
citation: Section 4.1 BM25 recall and retrieval coverage
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
With a 27,000-token context limit, BM25 retrieved a superset of oracle files in about 40% of instances, but in nearly half of instances it retrieved none of the oracle files.

## Source Excerpt

"We observe that in approximately 40% of instances, BM25 retrieves a superset of the oracle files for the 27,000-token context limit. However, in almost half of the instances with the 27,000-token limit, it retrieves none of the files from the 'oracle' context."

## Note

This evidence shows retrieval quality is uneven and introduces a direct caveat on context selection.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: result
citation: Section 5 oracle and oracle-collapsed results
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Claude 2 resolved 4.8% of issues with oracle retrieval and 5.9% with oracle-collapsed retrieval, both higher than its roughly 2% result under BM25 retrieval.

## Source Excerpt

"There, Claude 2 is able to resolve 4.8% of issues using the 'oracle' retriever." Later: "we see increases in performance... Claude 2 from 4.8% to 5.9%." Table 6 lists Claude 2 in the "Oracle"-collapsed setting with "Resolved 5.93".

## Note

These results indicate performance changes materially with more targeted context, but the oracle settings are less realistic than the main retrieval setup.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: result
citation: Section 5 context length analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Increasing context size improved BM25 recall relative to oracle files but reduced model performance, and the authors report that models often struggled to localize the relevant code within larger contexts.

## Source Excerpt

"We see that as total context length increases, Claude 2's performance drops considerably; behavior that is also observed in other models." "Even when increasing the maximum context size for BM25 would increase recall with respect to the oracle files, performance drops... as models are simply ineffective at localizing problematic code."

## Note

This is mechanism-relevant because it identifies a proposed failure mode, but it is bounded to the reported evaluation setting and author interpretation.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: method
citation: Section 3 SWE-Llama training details
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The SWE-Llama training set was built from 19,000 issue-PR pairs from 37 additional Python repositories, and LoRA fine-tuning excluded training sequences longer than 30,000 tokens, reducing the effective training corpus to 10,000 instances.

## Source Excerpt

"We follow our data collection procedure and collect 19,000 issue-PR pairs from an additional 37 popular Python package repositories." "For memory efficiency, we fine-tune only the weights of the attention sublayer using LoRA... and exclude training sequences with more than 30,000 tokens, reducing the effective size of the training corpus to 10,000 instances."

## Note

This evidence describes the open-model training data and truncation constraint used for fine-tuning.
---

evidence_count: 14
completed_at_utc: 2026-04-04T01:10:00Z

---

```markdown
---

SWE-bench issue descriptions average 195 words, non-test codebases average 3,010 files and 438K lines, and reference solutions average edits to 1.7 files, 3.0 functions, and 32.8 lines.

"Issue descriptions are typically long and detailed (195 words on average), and codebases regularly contain many thousands of files." These are summary statistics for benchmark instance size and edit scope.
```

```markdown
---

Each task instance has at least one fail-to-pass test, 40% of instances have at least two fail-to-pass tests, and the median instance runs 51 additional tests to check preserved functionality.

"For each task instance, there is at least one fail-to-pass test... and 40% of instances have at least two fail-to-pass tests." "In addition, a median of 51 additional tests run to check whether prior functionality is properly maintained."

These statistics describe the benchmark’s test coverage and preservation checks.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Table 5 SWE-bench baseline results
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Under BM25 retrieval on the full SWE-bench benchmark, Claude 2 resolved 1.97% of tasks, GPT-4-turbo 1.31%, ChatGPT-3.5 0.17%, SWE-Llama 7b 0.70%, and SWE-Llama 13b 0.70%.

## Source Excerpt

Table 5 reports for SWE-bench: "Claude 2 1.97", "ChatGPT-3.5 0.17", "GPT-4-turbo 1.31", "SWE-Llama 7b 0.70", and "SWE-Llama 13b 0.70" under "% Resolved."

## Note

The abstract rounds Claude 2’s result to 1.96%; Table 5 reports 1.97%.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: claim-support
citation: Section 5 best model performance
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper states that current evaluated models struggle broadly on SWE-bench, with the best-performing model solving only about 2% of issues under the main BM25 setting.

## Source Excerpt

"Across the board, models struggle significantly to resolve issues. The best performing model, Claude 2, is only able to resolve 1.96% of the issues."

## Note

This is a summary interpretation attached to reported benchmark results; the exact percentage varies slightly between abstract text and Table 5 (1.96% vs. 1.97%).
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: result
citation: Section 4.1 BM25 recall and retrieval coverage
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
With a 27,000-token context limit, BM25 retrieved a superset of oracle files in about 40% of instances, but in nearly half of instances it retrieved none of the oracle files.

## Source Excerpt

"We observe that in approximately 40% of instances, BM25 retrieves a superset of the oracle files for the 27,000-token context limit. However, in almost half of the instances with the 27,000-token limit, it retrieves none of the files from the 'oracle' context."

## Note

This evidence shows retrieval quality is uneven and introduces a direct caveat on context selection.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: result
citation: Section 5 oracle and oracle-collapsed results
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Claude 2 resolved 4.8% of issues with oracle retrieval and 5.9% with oracle-collapsed retrieval, both higher than its roughly 2% result under BM25 retrieval.

## Source Excerpt

"There, Claude 2 is able to resolve 4.8% of issues using the 'oracle' retriever." Later: "we see increases in performance... Claude 2 from 4.8% to 5.9%." Table 6 lists Claude 2 in the "Oracle"-collapsed setting with "Resolved 5.93".

## Note

These results indicate performance changes materially with more targeted context, but the oracle settings are less realistic than the main retrieval setup.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: result
citation: Section 5 context length analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Increasing context size improved BM25 recall relative to oracle files but reduced model performance, and the authors report that models often struggled to localize the relevant code within larger contexts.

## Source Excerpt

"We see that as total context length increases, Claude 2's performance drops considerably; behavior that is also observed in other models." "Even when increasing the maximum context size for BM25 would increase recall with respect to the oracle files, performance drops... as models are simply ineffective at localizing problematic code."

## Note

This is mechanism-relevant because it identifies a proposed failure mode, but it is bounded to the reported evaluation setting and author interpretation.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: method
citation: Section 3 SWE-Llama training details
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The SWE-Llama training set was built from 19,000 issue-PR pairs from 37 additional Python repositories, and LoRA fine-tuning excluded training sequences longer than 30,000 tokens, reducing the effective training corpus to 10,000 instances.

## Source Excerpt

"We follow our data collection procedure and collect 19,000 issue-PR pairs from an additional 37 popular Python package repositories." "For memory efficiency, we fine-tune only the weights of the attention sublayer using LoRA... and exclude training sequences with more than 30,000 tokens, reducing the effective size of the training corpus to 10,000 instances."

## Note

This evidence describes the open-model training data and truncation constraint used for fine-tuning.
---

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark-construction details, benchmark-scale statistics, evaluation protocol details, baseline model results, retrieval/context analyses, fine-tuning setup details, and stated limitations for SWE-bench as a software-engineering benchmark built from real GitHub issues and pull requests.

## Evidence Files

---
SWE-bench contains 2,294 software engineering task instances drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories.

## Source Excerpt

"We introduce SWE-bench, an evaluation framework consisting of 2,294 software engineering problems drawn from real GitHub issues and corresponding pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories."

## Note

This is a benchmark composition claim from the paper’s abstract and benchmark description.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Section 2.1 benchmark construction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench task construction uses a 3-stage pipeline: repo selection and PR scraping, attribute-based filtering, and execution-based filtering.

## Source Excerpt

"To find high-quality task instances at scale, we use a 3-stage pipeline as follows." The stages are then listed as "Stage I: Repo selection and data scraping", "Stage II: Attribute-based filtering", and "Stage III: Execution-based filtering."

## Note

The paper presents this as the core benchmark-construction method.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Section 2.1 repo scraping statistics
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The authors initially collected about 90,000 pull requests from 12 popular open-source Python repositories before filtering.

## Source Excerpt

"We start by collecting pull requests (PRs) from 12 popular open-source Python repositories on GitHub, producing about ∼90,000 PRs in total."

## Note

This describes the pre-filtering scale of the raw source data.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-023
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Section 2.1 attribute and execution filtering
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Candidate tasks were limited to merged pull requests that resolve a GitHub issue, modify test files, include at least one fail-to-pass test, and do not produce installation or runtime errors.

## Source Excerpt

"Stage II: Attribute-based filtering. We create candidate tasks by selecting the merged PRs that (1) resolve a GitHub issue and (2) make changes to the test files of the repository." "We filter out task instances without at least one test where its status changes from a fail to pass... We also filter out instances that result in installation or runtime errors."

## Note

This combines the explicit filtering criteria across stages II and III.
```
